The Department of Revenue estimates the incentives cost the state ~$38M/year. A two-thirds supermajority is required to repeal a tax incentive, making passage difficult despite some bipartisan support. Gov. Hobbs called for repeal in her State of the State address and proposed a penny-per-gallon fee on data center water use ($6.5M/year for water conservation). The exemption costs $38.5M/year (up from $1.4M in 2020, projected to reach $60M+ by FY2027).

SB2653 creates the Tennessee Data Center Impact Review Act, establishing a formal review process for data center impacts on communities, environment, and infrastructure. The bill amends multiple Tennessee Code sections across eight titles and was introduced February 2, 2026 with companion bill HB2392. It was deferred in the Senate Commerce and Labor Committee to March 17, 2026, while HB2392 was placed on the Agriculture and Natural Resources Subcommittee calendar for March 18.

Wisconsin AB 840 / SB 843 (Rep. Shannon Zimmerman R-River Falls, Sen. Romaine Quinn R-Birchwood) — a GOP-led data center bill — passed the Assembly in January 2026 by a 53-44 mostly party-line vote (2 Democrats yes, 1 Republican no). The bill would have prevented data center operators from passing energy costs onto residential consumers, required them to pay for their own generation/transmission infrastructure, mandated closed-loop water systems, required annual water use reporting, and required reclamation bonds. However, the Wisconsin Senate adjourned without taking up AB-840 — or any data center regulation bill — on the floor. Democrats and several construction unions opposed the bill due to lack of prevailing wage requirements and concerns over energy consumption. The competing Democratic Data Center Accountability Act (SB 729/AB 722) by Sen. Habush Sinykin and Rep. Stroud, requiring 70% renewable sourcing and a new very-large-customer rate class, was heard in the Senate Utilities Committee on Feb 17, 2026 but never advanced.

Wisconsin SB 729 / AB 722, the Data Center Accountability Act, authored by Sen. Jodi Habush Sinykin (D-Whitefish Bay) and Rep. Angela Stroud (D-Ashland), would require data centers to source at least 70% of electricity from renewables to qualify for state tax exemptions, create a 'very large customers' rate class with rate cases every 2 years, and provide transparency on water and energy use. SB 729 was introduced Dec 2, 2025 and got a Senate Utilities Committee hearing Feb 17, 2026. AB 722 (the Assembly companion) was introduced Dec 3, 2025 and is awaiting a hearing. Neither bill advanced before the Senate adjourned, leaving Wisconsin without statewide data center regulation.

Indiana HB 1210 was signed into law March 12, 2026 by Gov. Braun. The data center incentive provision was drastically watered down in private meetings between legislators and data center lobbyists. What was pitched as 1% of electricity costs (potentially $5M/year for a large facility) was cut to at most 1/14th of that — 'not more than' one penny per dollar of the 7% sales tax exemption, capping payments at ~$350K/year instead of the original $5M minimum. The final deal essentially gutted community benefit payments while preserving the industry's tax breaks.

Ohio House passed HB 646 on March 18, 2026, creating a 13-member Data Center Study Commission to examine impacts on environment, electrical grid, consumer utility rates, farmland, local economy, noise, and light pollution. Report due within 6 months. Now heads to Ohio Senate. The House also delayed a vote on overriding the data center tax exemption.

A Hopkins County District Court judge issued a temporary injunction against power system construction at the Sulphur Springs AI data center site, preserving 5,000 acres at the heart of a lawsuit. Vistra Corp./Luminant Mining, former landowners, argue the city breached deed restrictions prohibiting energy production/storage on the land. The judge denied the city's motion to dismiss. The Texas AG sided with the city, but construction remains halted pending resolution.

Planning and Zoning Commission voted 4-3 to reject a 936-acre hyperscale AI data center zoning change from Low Density Single Family to General Industrial, requested by Hugo Reed and Associates on behalf of Texas Solar Works. The site is off Northeast Loop 289, and the facility would require significant power from ERCOT lines, solar, and gas. About 10 north and east Lubbock residents objected to the project, citing environmental burdens on neighborhoods already surrounded by industrial neighbors. The developer withdrew and plans to revise the proposal to better align with community feedback.

San Antonio City Council is pursuing zoning code amendments to regulate data center growth. The Southwest Workers Union organized a 'Data Center Rebellion Convening' where over 60 organizers from across Texas gathered for two days to share tactics in fighting data centers. Organizers from San Marcos (successfully blocked a $1.5B DC), Taylor, and Tucson shared strategies. The convening reflects a growing coordinated grassroots movement across Texas. Mayor Gina Ortiz Jones supports expedited regulation.
opposition_groups: San Antonio Residents Concerned About Data Centers

properties.summary: Misae Investments' Mojo Ranch project proposes a renewable energy and data infrastructure hub on 2,742 acres near Stockdale, including utility-scale solar, battery energy storage, and AI data centers. Site work with tree clearing has already begun. Commercial operation is targeted for June 2028. A new group, Protect Wilson County Water, formed in February 2026 with nearly 150 residents attending a meeting to discuss water use, air emissions, and lack of transparency. Developer Miguel Oneto cited Wilson County's grid interconnection potential and 'welcoming community.'

EdgeConneX (Herndon, VA, working with Rackspace Technology) is building a $1.4B+ data center campus in Cedar Creek (Bastrop County, TX) at FM 535 and Wolf Lane. First building of 4 (578,000 sq ft, $440M) targeted for June 2026 completion. Project DFW33220N total: 2.8M sq ft over 5-8 years. As of late March 2026, EdgeConneX announced plans for a SECOND data center campus in Bastrop County on a 180-acre property at 6682 FM 535 in Cedar Creek. Bastrop County hired PFM Group Consulting in March 2026 ($109K not-to-exceed) to analyze the long-term impact on the county's tax base. Residents continue to weigh impacts.

Cascade Township Board voted unanimously on February 26, 2026 for a 6-month moratorium on data center permits, with an option for a 6-month extension. The action was preemptive — no data center proposal was pending — as a growing conversation about data center development spread across West Michigan. The board created a subcommittee to lead fact-finding on data center impacts and study zoning amendments, amid concerns the township lacked specific protections against large-scale data center facilities.
opposition_groups: Economic Development Responsibility Alliance of Michigan

properties.summary: In Gaines Charter Township, Michigan, Microsoft seeking to rezone 356 acres (purchased from Steelcase for $45.3M) for data center. Dec 2025 hearing canceled when 250+ overflowed venue. Microsoft terminated NDAs with Gaines and 3 other West MI communities in March 2026. Planning Commission hearing expected April. A petition gathered 415 signatures. Key concerns driving this action include community impacts. The community successfully achieved its objective.

In Florida, SB 484 would establish a regulatory framework for hyperscale data centers. Requires PSC to ensure data centers pay their own utility costs. Prohibits certain NDAs. Preserves local government land use authority. Passed both chambers March 13, 2026 in weakened form; signed by Governor DeSantis, effective July 1, 2026. Major provisions include separate utility rate class and transparency requirements. Governor DeSantis signed SB 484 on March 24. Effective July 1, 2026.

In Newton County, Georgia, Board of Commissioners unanimously enacted 30-day moratorium on data center applications. Existing ordinance limits DCs to Stanton Springs overlay, but development creeping into incorporated areas. Moratorium extended through April 8, 2026 (originally 30 days). Staff reviewing development ordinances. Data centers have crept into incorporated areas like Covington, Social Circle, and Porterdale through city annexation.

MDEQ Permit Board granted air permit for 41 gas turbines powering xAI's Colossus 2 on March 10, 2026, despite 300+ opponents at the February hearing and zero public support. 27 turbines were already operating without permits. On April 9, 2026, the Southern Environmental Law Center filed an appeal on behalf of NAACP, Young Gifted & Green, and Safe and Sound Coalition challenging the permit. The appeal argues the permit fails to show pollution won't harm air quality, underestimates emissions, doesn't require offsets, and ignores xAI's existing operation of unpermitted turbines. The facility would be the largest industrial NOx source in the 11-county Memphis metro area.

In Louisiana, LPSC refused to investigate Meta's sale of 80% of its $27B Richland Parish data center to Blue Owl Capital. Meta retains only 20% stake with a 4-year lease. Earthjustice/UCS warned ratepayers could be stuck paying for 3 new Entergy gas plants if Meta exits. The project involves 2250 acres. Opposition was led by Alliance for Affordable Energy and Union of Concerned Scientists. Key concerns driving this action include energy grid strain. The outcome remains pending.

Hut 8's $10B River Bend AI data center campus in West Feliciana Parish received zoning approval from the Parish Planning and Zoning Commission (611 acres). Phase I: a 245 MW facility leased to Fluidstack for $7B over 15 years, with Fluidstack holding a right of first offer for up to 1,000 MW additional capacity. Hut 8 secured 330 MW initial utility power from Entergy Louisiana. Construction is underway with operations expected Q2 2027. The deal could net the parish $90M annually.

HR 6984, the Data Center Transparency Act, was introduced January 8, 2026 by Rep. Robert Menendez (NJ) and Rep. Greg Casar (TX), referred to House Energy and Commerce Committee. Requires EPA to submit quarterly reports to Congress on data center water and energy use (including reuse, pollution discharge, impacts on local water supplies); requires the EIA to collect total energy consumption data every 6 months for each U.S. data center and report to Congress. Introduced alongside the PRICE (Preventing Rate Inflation in Consumer Energy) Act. As of early 2026, bill remains in committee but transparency-focused legislation faces less industry opposition than substantive regulation.

Georgia HB 559 would accelerate the sunset date for the state's data center sales and use tax exemption program, ending tax breaks for new data centers by 2026 rather than the originally scheduled later date. The bill is part of a wave of Georgia legislation targeting data center incentives in the 2026 session, alongside SB 410 (which passed the Senate to repeal exemptions entirely) and HB 1063 (which passed the House 159-5 requiring data centers to pay infrastructure costs).

SB 408, sponsored by Sen. Nan Orrock (D-Atlanta), would move the sunset date for Georgia's data center sales and use tax exemption from January 1, 2032 to January 1, 2027, effectively ending the incentive five years early. It is one of several bills introduced in the 2025-26 session targeting the exemption, including SB 410 (immediate repeal) and HB 559 (sunset December 31, 2026). A state audit found the exemption has cost Georgia significant revenue while creating relatively few jobs per dollar of tax benefit.

In Idaho, H 820 would revise data center sales tax and property tax exemptions. Requires qualifying businesses to receive electricity under rate schedules that fully recover all utility costs. Mandates written notice to local water providers and Department of Water Resources detailing anticipated water consumption needs.

Idaho HB 756 (Rep. Stephanie Mickelsen R-Idaho Falls), introduced February 2026, would prohibit the Idaho PUC from approving any new customer requiring 20+ MW of electricity from the state's three regulated power companies without a 'no harm' test proving it would not require raising rates for other customers. The test analyzes grid reliability impacts, infrastructure needs, and risks. The bill raises the large-load threshold from 10 MW to 20 MW and exempts longstanding high-power consumers (existing customers with >50 MW demand as of Jan 1, 2000, like Micron Technology). HB 756 received House State Affairs Committee approval to be introduced in late February but had not yet been scheduled for a public hearing as of late February 2026. A separate bill on data center water consumption has also advanced.

Florida SB 1517, 'Approval of Data Center Facilities,' sponsored by Rep. Joseph, required developers to disclose projected energy, water, carbon emissions, noise, and environmental impacts at application stage, with the Department of Environmental Protection publishing the data publicly. The bill was filed January 9, 2026 and referred to the Economic Infrastructure Subcommittee, State Affairs, and Commerce committees, but died in the Economic Infrastructure Subcommittee on March 13, 2026.

Ohio Republican legislative leaders are pushing to override Governor DeWine's veto that repealed data center sales tax exemptions. The proposed override via HB 96 would restore the tax break that DeWine eliminated. The Ohio Farm Bureau issued an action alert urging members to challenge the override, arguing data centers should not receive preferential tax treatment while consuming vast amounts of farmland, water, and electricity. Ohioans for Responsible Development organized opposition to the override effort.

In New Jersey, S 731 was reintroduced after a Murphy pocket veto. Requires utilities to develop tariff for data centers using 100+ MW monthly. New large load customers must post financial guarantees for 85% of requested service for 10+ years. Reported from Senate Committee with amendments. Key concerns driving this action include ratepayer cost concerns. The outcome remains pending.

CoreWeave received the first award under New Jersey's Next New Jersey AI program: a $250M tax credit ($50M/year for 5 years) for a nearly $1.8B, 250MW facility at the former Merck campus in Kenilworth, purchased for $322 million in 2024 (27 acres). Critics note the program lacks the community benefits agreement requirements mandated by older NJ incentive programs like Aspire and Emerge for projects over $10M. NJ Policy Perspective questioned why the state needs to subsidize a fast-growing AI industry with hundreds of billions in capital investment.

In Maryland, HB 1411 would require large-scale data center operators to report environmental and resource impacts publicly. Mandates jurisdictions with 10,000+ residents create formal data center management plans by 2027. Includes $1,000/MW payment into EmPOWER program for low-income assistance. The outcome remains pending. The community's stated objective was to mandate public environmental reporting from data centers (HB 1411).

Maryland HB1595 authorizes Baltimore City and county governing bodies to establish a special personal property tax subclass for qualified data centers and set a rate up to 2.5x the standard real property tax rate. The bill emerged from Frederick County's push for local taxing authority over data center equipment after the county discovered data centers' vast server farms were taxed at the same rate as other business personal property. The legislation was introduced February 15, 2026 and referred to the Ways and Means Committee.

Frederick County Delegate Jesse Pippy introduced a bill proposing a data center moratorium specifically for Frederick County while the state conducts a comprehensive study of data center impacts. The legislation responds to growing community concern after the Frederick County Council voted 5-2 to adopt a 2,615-acre 'Critical Digital Infrastructure' overlay zone, which prompted residents to launch a referendum petition campaign that gathered over 22,000 signatures. Maryland counties more broadly have been calling on the data center industry to slow down.
opposition_groups: Frederick County Data Center Referendum Committee

properties.summary: In Pennsylvania, voluntary executive program offering expedited state permitting for data center developers who commit to bringing/paying for their own power generation, transparency and community engagement, Hiring local workers, and environmental protection including water conservation. Critics note standards are voluntary with no enforcement.

City of Industry's Planning Commission voted in December 2025 to recommend zoning changes allowing data centers citywide (proposed by EdgeCore) (62 acres). City Manager Joshua Nelson wrote in May 2025 that the city was 'working on a change to our code to pretty much allow data centers anywhere.' With fewer than 300 residents, the city faced minimal public opposition. Majestic Realty pitched the Industry Business Center campus for mid-sized (20-80 MW) data centers. A formal proposal for IBC Building 10 was submitted in late 2025.

In City of Oakley, California, Oakley City Council approved the 164-acre Bridgehead Industrial Project on March 11, 2026 in a 4-1 vote, but with an amendment excluding data centers after sustained community opposition. Residents raised concerns about water and electricity demands at public hearings and Planning Commission meetings. On February 9, 2026, the developer (Oxfoot Oakley) agreed to remove data centers from the proposal. The project was approved as a logistics/industrial hub without data center uses.

In City of El Monte, California, On March 12, 2026, the City Council postponed action on data center moratorium evaluation after a verbal altercation during public comment led police to evacuate the chamber. Debate rescheduled to March 18, 2026. City Council unanimously passed a 45-day moratorium on March 19, 2026. Opposition was led by El Monte Neighbors United. Key concerns driving this action include community impacts. The community successfully achieved its objective.

SB 978, the Data Center Community Accountability Act, introduced by Sen. Sasha Renée Pérez (D-Pasadena) on February 4, 2026. Bans new diesel backup generators at data centers in high-pollution zones, mandating clean technologies (hydrogen fuel cells or long-duration battery storage). Requires the PUC to establish a special rate class for large-scale energy users (75+ MW) to prevent cost shifts to other ratepayers. Directs the California Energy Commission to study impacts of large data center loads on decarbonization goals. Mandates prevailing wages and local skilled workforce for hyperscale construction.

Multiple lawsuits surround data center development in Imperial Valley. The City of Imperial sued Imperial County in December 2025 to force environmental review of a $10B data center by Imperial Valley Computer Manufacturing LLC. In February 2026, the court ruled the city's CEQA claims were insufficient. In April 2026, the developer filed defamation and civil rights lawsuits against the City of Imperial and environmental activists. On April 2, 2026, a judge denied Imperial's ex parte application to block a county board vote on a lot merger. A case management conference is set for June 2, 2026. Separately, CalEthos is developing a 330 MW, 315-acre project near Niland/Calipatria.

Federal class action lawsuit (Pearson v. Port of Morrow) against Amazon, Port of Morrow and 17 total defendants over nitrate contamination of 634+ domestic drinking water wells in Morrow County, where Amazon operates 13 data centers. On March 31, 2026, Amazon agreed to pay $20.5 million to settle, becoming the first Big Tech company to commit to paying damages related to public health threats from its data centers. Amazon denied any wrongdoing. The settlement funds will support private well and public infrastructure projects. Litigation continues against the remaining defendants including Port of Morrow, Lamb Weston, Madison Ranches, Threemile Canyon Farms, PGE, and Columbia River Processing.

Governor Tina Kotek formally announced the Data Center Advisory Committee on January 20, 2026, with seven members co-chaired by Margaret Hoffmann (NW Power and Conservation Council) and Michael Jung. The committee includes Umatilla County commissioner Dan Dorran, U of O professor Greg Dotson, and energy/business professionals. It will hold at least one public meeting per month for six months, with policy recommendations due to the Governor and Legislature by October 2026. Focus areas include regulations for responsible siting, energy and water supply impacts, and protecting low-income ratepayers.

The Van Hollen Power to the People Act directs FERC to mandate grid operators create specific queues for data center electricity connection requests, encourages states to establish rate classes specific to data centers, and requires data center operators to cover the full cost of generation, transmission, and distribution upgrades. Includes prevailing wage and registered apprenticeship requirements.

In New Jersey, Gov. Mikie Sherrill signed on her first day in office, Declaring a state of emergency on utility costs. Directs BPU to require electric utilities to report on energy requests from data centers ('ghost loads'). Identifies data center demand surge as key driver of NJ's energy crisis. Key concerns driving this action include tax incentive policy and water consumption. The outcome remains pending.

Public Utility Commission of Texas conducting a survey of water use by data centers and virtual currency mining facilities. Authorized by state budget rider from Rep. Armando Walle. Survey asks about direct water use, cooling technology, and indirect water use. Results shared with Texas Water Development Board and TCEQ, due to legislature by end of 2026. Texas has 464 data centers with 70+ under development.

Earthjustice and a coalition of 9 groups challenged the Montana PSC's protective order shielding NorthWestern Energy's data center plans (letters of intent for up to 1,400 MW to 3 developers: Atlas Power, Sabey, Quantica). On March 31, 2026, the PSC ruled NorthWestern doesn't have to turn over additional data center information in its merger case, a setback for transparency advocates. Groups argue Montana's Constitution guarantees the public right to know about utility plans affecting ratepayers.

Harlingen city commissioners voted unanimously on April 2, 2026 to begin the process for a 120-day data center moratorium, the first in the Rio Grande Valley. Two public hearings expected in May and June. The city plans to work with WaterWorks to evaluate water supply impacts, wastewater capacity, and electrical load demands. Driven by months of community pushback over strained infrastructure from proposed data center developments.

New Castle County Council unanimously passed first-of-their-kind data center regulations after 7 months of debate. Requires 1,000-foot buffer from residential zones (reducible to 500 ft with noise study), bans open-loop water cooling (unless using reclaimed water). Notably exempts projects already in the development pipeline, including Starwood's Project Washington. Effective March 19, 2026.

SB 205, sponsored by Sen. Hansen, requires businesses using 100+ MW of electricity (originally 30 MW, amended after industry pushback) to obtain a certificate to operate from the Delaware Public Service Commission. Certificates could mandate grid modernization payments or additional energy generation commitments. The bill remains tabled as Sen. Hansen works through business community concerns and assesses whether sufficient support exists. Delaware could see five new data centers that would double the state's total electricity usage.

QTS Data Centers is undertaking a multi-phase expansion of its 194-acre East Windsor, New Jersey, campus on Princeton Hightstown Road, formerly occupied by McGraw-Hill, with plans for 554,000 square feet of modernized facilities. Residents from the Twin Rivers community packed Planning Board hearings to challenge the expansion, raising concerns about water consumption, energy grid strain, and environmental risks from the facility's growing footprint.

HR 6529 (Protecting Families from Power Price Spikes Act) directs FERC to convene a technical conference within 90 days on how large electricity loads — especially AI data centers — affect residential and small commercial customers' bills. FERC must submit a report with recommendations to Congressional energy committees within 180 days. The outcome remains pending.

The Seneca County Board of Supervisors voted unanimously in March 2026 to join Tompkins County in asking the New York DEC to reject TeraWulf's modified water permit for its proposed 400MW AI data center at a former coal plant site on Cayuga Lake in Lansing (183 acres). TeraWulf seeks to withdraw over one million gallons of water per day for cooling, with returned water at elevated temperatures that critics say would encourage harmful algal blooms threatening drinking water and tourism. Local groups have also filed a lawsuit against TeraWulf and the Lansing zoning board.

After Natelli Investments withdrew the New Hill Digital Campus application (300MW, 189 acres) on March 5 due to intense community opposition from the Protect Wake County Coalition, the Apex Town Council voted unanimously on March 10 to draft a moratorium. A public hearing is scheduled for April 14, 2026 at 6 p.m. at Apex Town Hall. A one-year moratorium is expected to be approved. Mayor Jacques Gilbert also proposed a non-standing committee to research data center impacts.

In Hanover County, Virginia, Developer Tract proposed the Mountain Road Tech Park — a 427-acre, 900-MW data center campus along Route 33 and Mountain Road near the Hanover-Henrico line (430 acres). The project would include five buildings, requiring up to 600,000 gallons/day of water. The Planning Commission unanimously deferred the proposal. It is being reconsidered at the March 19, 2026 meeting. Planning Commission deferred again on March 19 (second deferral). Rescheduled to April 16.

In Wise County, Virginia, Red Post Energy Group signed a letter of intent with Wise, Virginia, Innovation Hub Venture (OASIS) to develop the Maverick Project — a phased natural gas power plant for a data center complex adjacent to Lonesome Pine Regional Industrial Facility (300 acres). The project comprises nine buildings totaling 2 million sq ft with 600 MW capacity and an on-site gas power plant. Appalachian Voices opposes the project. The complex would create 500-700 construction jobs and 30-50 operations jobs per building.

The Moore County, Tennessee, Metro Council is considering a two-year moratorium on data center construction and expansion, which would take effect April 20, 2026. During the moratorium, the county would stop accepting new applications for special use permits, zoning amendments, and building permits related to data centers. County staff would study potential effects on energy use, water demand, noise, and land use in coordination with local utility providers.

Three citizen groups -- Tucker United, West Virginia Highlands Conservancy, and the Sierra Club -- filed an appeal challenging the state Air Quality Board's decision to uphold an air quality permit for the proposed Ridgeline power plant and data center in Tucker County, located between Davis and Thomas (proposed by Fundamental Data LLC). The 500-acre facility would include a natural gas power plant fueling a data center. The appeal argues the WVDEP wrongly classified the facility as a 'synthetic minor source' rather than a 'major source' of emissions.

Vermont H.727, the Sustainable Data Centers Act, sponsored by Rep. Laura Sibilia (I-Dover), regulates 'factory-sized developments' requiring 20+ MW power capacity. Focuses on proper siting, environmental protections, and ratepayer cost controls but applies the same requirements to all data centers regardless of technology or environmental footprint. A companion Senate bill, S.205, would ban data centers over 100 MW until July 2030 pending a Public Utility Commission study.

Rhode Island S2346 establishes 30-year property, tangible, sales and use tax exemptions for qualified data centers. Proposed by Sen. DiPalma (D), who also works as a technical director at Raytheon. Critics argued tech companies would build data centers without incentives; supporters noted 42 other states already offer sales-tax exemptions. Senate Commerce Committee recommended held for further study on March 10 after nearly an hour of floor debate.

Rhode Island S2776 requires data centers needing large amounts of electricity to fund the cost of necessary infrastructure improvements to supply that power, rather than passing costs to existing ratepayers. Rhode Island currently hosts seven data centers, none of which would meet the proposed threshold. Senate Commerce Committee recommended held for further study on March 10, 2026, alongside companion tax incentive bill S2346.

Maine LD 713 would exclude data centers from Maine's Business Equipment Tax Exemption (BETE) program (100% property tax break) and the Dirigo Business Incentives Programs. The bill also requires the Maine DECD to study other state incentives data centers may benefit from. Supported by the Natural Resources Council of Maine, the bill was presented to the Taxation Committee on March 12, 2026. The bill is pending committee action.

Sentinel Data Centers has an agreement to occupy 1 million square feet on two floors of a former paper mill in Jay, Maine, employing 800-1,000 construction workers and 125 permanent staff. The project faces an existential threat from LD 307, a bill advancing in the Maine Legislature that would impose a two-year moratorium on data centers above 20MW. Notably, the developer has not encountered local opposition from Jay residents, partly because the project does not seek special tax breaks.

Northern New England Energy Company is proposing the 'Sanford Woods Industrial and Technical Campus,' a 100-300MW data center covering over 1,000 acres along the Mousam River in Sanford. The developer claims the facility would generate its own power using fuel cells, solar, and battery storage without burdening Maine's grid. The project is threatened by LD 307, a proposed state moratorium on data centers above 20MW. Local residents have raised concerns about noise, environmental damage, and the significant changes a facility of this scale would bring.
opposition_groups: Sanford Residents Concerned About Data Centers

properties.summary: After the NE Edge/Millstone nuclear data center project was scrapped, Governor Lamont signaled Connecticut may require new data centers to generate their own power. The state's high electricity prices have made it less attractive for data centers. Only one company has used CT's data center tax incentive since 2021. No moratorium bills introduced.

A massive estimated $10 billion data center project backed by Georgia Lt. Gov. Burt Jones and his family is proposed for Butts County as part of the Highway 16 Interstate Health Development near I-75, which would include 11 million square feet of data centers. The project is expected to consume over 4.5 million gallons of water per day, more than tripling Butts County's current water usage. Georgia Attorney General Chris Carr accused Jones of using his office to benefit his family's property interests, and environmental advocates have flagged the lack of available information about the facility's cooling technology and energy demands.

Alabama SB265, sponsored by Sen. Andrew Jones (R-Centre), limits data center tax abatement periods to 20 years starting January 1, 2027, down from the current 30-year maximum. The bill also mandates that large-scale data centers consuming 100+ MW pay state sales and use taxes. The governor may waive taxes for economically struggling counties. Passed the Senate 30-0 on March 13, 2026, with Governor Kay Ivey's support as part of a broader package aimed at lowering costs for Alabama families.

In Alabama, SB 354 would impose a one-year moratorium on construction of new large-scale solar power facilities, Prompted by opposition to a 4,500-acre solar project in Stockton meant to power Meta's Montgomery data center expansion. Exempts TVA solar capacity and projects already under construction. Opposition was led by Stockton Area Residents Against Solar. Key concerns driving this action include environmental impacts and loss of farmland. The outcome remains pending.

Following Governor Jeff Landry's announcement of Amazon's $12 billion investment in three interconnected data center campuses across Caddo, Louisiana, and Bossier parishes, Commissioner Chris Kracman proposed a one-year moratorium on developments using water from Caddo Lake, but the resolution failed to receive a second from any other commissioner. Amazon has pledged to limit cooling water use to less than 13% of the year and invest $400 million in public water systems in the Caddo-Bossier area.

As part of Amazon's $12 billion Northwest Louisiana data center investment, STACK Infrastructure is developing a 1,089-acre 'Highway 3 Technology Park' north of Benton in Bossier Parish, planned for over 1.3 million square feet across six buildings. The Bossier Parish Police Jury scheduled a public hearing for March 18, 2026 -- the first opportunity for public comment since the project was confirmed. Residents have raised concerns about air quality, proximity to homes, water supply, and rising utility costs.
opposition_groups: Benton Area Residents Concerned About Data Centers

properties.summary: Florida SB 1118 creates a public records exemption allowing local governments to keep data center siting plans confidential for up to 12 months when requested by the company. A developer's 'proprietary confidential business information' remains exempt until it becomes public or the company stops treating it as proprietary. Includes a 5-year sunset provision expiring October 2031. The bill was enacted alongside companion SB 484, which establishes a comprehensive regulatory framework for hyperscale data centers effective July 1, 2026.

UPDATE: Monticello residents rallied against two proposed data centers (600+ acres combined, 4M+ sq ft). An online petition exceeded 1,200 signatures. Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy filed a lawsuit against the city challenging the review process. A Facebook opposition group quickly gained traction. Residents gathered at local parks to distribute yard signs.
opposition_groups: Stop the Monticello Data Centers MN

properties.summary: CloudHQ's 1.4M sq ft data center at West Creek Corporate Center received preliminary approval from the Chaska, Minnesota, City Council on October 21, 2024, with a 4-1 vote. The lone dissenter was Ward 2 representative Taylor Hubbard. Residents voiced concerns about the 72.5-foot building height (double the 36-foot limit), noise, and neighborhood character impacts. Planning Commission raised issues about unprecedented height and landscaping requirements. Final site plan approval is pending future Planning Commission and City Council review.

The Warrenton, Missouri, Board of Aldermen voted unanimously in January 2026 to approve a conditional use permit for a Crusoe data center on a 340-acre parcel, consisting of two 805,000-square-foot buildings and a 40,000-square-foot administration building. More than 40 residents spoke both for and against the proposal, with opponents raising concerns about noise, light pollution, water supply impacts on aquifers, and the pace of the approval process.

Gateway Digital Campus and Provident Data Centers are seeking to rezone roughly 600 acres of agricultural land near I-44 and Robertsville Road in Villa Ridge, Missouri, for a hyperscale data center, sitting directly adjacent to the Shaw Nature Reserve. The project returned to the Franklin County Planning & Zoning Commission on March 17, 2026 despite being tabled for 6 months in January. Both Gateway and the Beltline Energy project are on the same March 17 agenda, alongside proposed county-wide data center regulations including 400ft setbacks, 800-acre site limits, and sound limits.

The city of Osawatomie, Kansas, entered a pre-development agreement with Alcove Development/Northland Data Centers for a $900 million to $1.1 billion data center project on 115 acres of city-owned land, requiring one million gallons of water and 150MW of power per day. Residents have pushed back, citing the city's aging water infrastructure, fears of noise, and lack of community input. The Miami County Commission is considering a temporary moratorium countywide while proper regulations are developed.
opposition_groups: Osawatomie Residents Concerned About Data Centers

properties.summary: LB1261 was signed April 10, 2026, allowing private developers to build and own power plants to serve large-scale electricity users like data centers, and prohibiting Nebraska's public power utilities from using eminent domain to acquire such plants. Connected to Google's proposed Nebraska data center that could require 1,000-3,000 MW (3x Lincoln's peak demand). The Legislature passed it 34-7 despite opposition arguing it fast-tracks a new business model threatening Nebraska's land, water, and utility rates.

The Troy, Illinois, Planning Commission approved a six-month moratorium on data processing centers in March 2026, following the collapse of Cloverleaf Infrastructure's proposal for a 1.4-million-square-foot hyperscale data center sited less than a mile from neighborhoods, daycares, and senior living facilities. Hundreds of residents packed a meeting at Triad High School in February to challenge the project. The moratorium prevents new data center applications while the planning commission strengthens zoning ordinances. Troy Planning Commission approved a six-month moratorium on data processing centers on March 13, after Cloverleaf Infrastructure's 500MW proposal collapsed following massive public opposition. Hundreds of residents packed Triad High School in February. City Council final passage expected March 16.
opposition_groups: Say NO to Data Centers in Troy IL

properties.summary: HydraVault received a full building permit from Chicago, Illinois, to construct a 76,000-square-foot, 20-megawatt AI data center at 2538 S. Wabash Avenue in the South Loop. Residents reported opposition due to energy consumption, water use, and the lack of public hearings — the project was classified as a 'minor change' by zoning officials, bypassing community review.

After a nearly six-hour contentious city council meeting, Yorkville, Illinois, approved Pioneer Development's 'Project Cardinal' — a 1,037-acre, 1.8-gigawatt data center campus with 14 buildings totaling 17 million square feet, plus a $51 million upfront payment. Residents spoke against it, citing water usage, noise, air quality, and property value concerns. Yorkville City Council approved Project Cardinal at 12:51 AM on March 12 after a nearly 6-hour contentious meeting. The 1,037-acre campus (Pioneer Development LLC) includes 14 buildings totaling 17M sq ft. Development agreement includes $51M in upfront payments over 4 years and $68.25M directed to the school district ($91M total). First data center operational by 2029.
opposition_groups: Block Project Steel and Project Cardinal

properties.summary: Cloverleaf Infrastructure has been in detailed discussions with Edwardsville, Illinois, officials since mid-2025 about a data center south of New Poag Road. Obtained emails revealed extensive behind-the-scenes planning despite public assurances of 'no formal proposal.' Residents for Responsible Growth organized a meeting of nearly 300 people in March 2026.

Sprigg Township, Ohio, trustees passed a 12-month voluntary moratorium on data centers in late February 2026, in response to a proposed facility near the former Stuart power plant requiring 1,300 megawatts — 31 times Adams County's annual power consumption. First of two Adams County localities to act, followed by Monroe Township.

Ashville, Ohio, Village Council unanimously passed a 180-day emergency moratorium on December 20, 2025, in response to EdgeConneX's proposal on 680 acres including an on-site 200-megawatt natural gas power plant. Residents lined yards with 'No Data Center' signs. EdgeConneX withdrew its annexation application but reportedly already holds land zoned for data center use.

Columbus City Council held hearings in March 2026 examining rapid data center growth in central Ohio, with large facilities potentially requiring up to 5 million gallons of water per day. Of 130 data centers in the area, only 18 fall within city limits, limiting regulatory authority. Some councilmembers called for a moratorium though no legislation has been introduced.

Senate Bill 969, authored by Sen. Andre Jacque (R-New Franken), would prohibit NDAs between data center companies and municipalities, while still allowing withholding of genuine trade secrets. The bill was prompted by a Wisconsin Watch investigation revealing at least four communities had signed secrecy deals hiding billion-dollar data center plans. Rep. Clint Moses (R-Menomonie), author of the companion Assembly bill, called the measure 'really about trust.' Passed Senate Utilities Committee 4-1, but the Senate adjourned the 2026 session without a floor vote.

Tulsa, Oklahoma, City Councilor Laura Bellis proposed a 365-day moratorium on new data center construction citywide (by Meta / Beale Infrastructure). The ordinance would pause acceptance, processing, and issuance of building permits for data centers while a working group studies impacts. The moratorium was heard at the March 11 council meeting and a final vote is scheduled for March 25, 2026. Bellis says not all colleagues are on board. The moratorium would not affect existing Project Anthem ($800M, 340 acres in east Tulsa). Bellis is open to amendments including a shorter duration. Tulsa City Council passed the moratorium unanimously on March 25, amended to 9 months (through Dec 31, 2026). Exempts Project Anthem phases 1-2 and Project Clydesdale.

SB 1488, sponsored by Republican Sen. Sacchieri, would have imposed a three-year moratorium on data centers with electrical load greater than 100 MW until November 1, 2029. Directed the Oklahoma Corporation Commission to study impacts on water supply, utility rates, property values, and ideal siting locations. The bill missed key legislative deadlines and died without advancing from committee.

The Mat-Su Borough Assembly plan to help a private company find investors/developers for a large data center on borough-owned land was vetoed by Mayor Edna DeVries, but the Assembly voted 6-1 to override the veto on March 19, 2026. More than a dozen people testified against data centers, while about three spoke in support. Opponents cited energy costs (14-17 cents/kWh in Southcentral), tightening Cook Inlet gas supply, and environmental concerns.

Shreveport, Louisiana, Metropolitan Planning Commission denied a special use permit for a 2.8 million sq ft Franks Investment Company LLC data center at 7340 Greenwood Road (Resilient Technology Park) on a 4-4 vote on December 3, 2025, after major community backlash over noise, water use (7.5M gallons/day), and environmental impacts. The Shreveport City Council overturned the denial on December 18, 2025 in a unanimous 7-0 vote, and separately approved selling up to 7.5M gallons of water daily to the facility. A subsequent lawsuit was filed accusing the city of violating constitutional obligations.

Hermiston, Oregon, City Council voted in September 2025 to annex 800 acres of land to attract more data centers, with only one councilor (Jackie Linton) voting against. Linton raised concerns about water supply impacts and inadequate review time. Amazon Web Services already has dozens of data centers across Umatilla and Morrow counties and is building two more in Hermiston. The annexation was approved over community objections.

Oklahoma HB 2992, the Data Center Consumer Ratepayer Protection Act, passed the Oklahoma House 92-2 with overwhelming bipartisan support on March 23, 2026. The bill defines 'large load customers' as new facilities adding 75+ MW and sets guidelines for how electric suppliers address rising energy demands from data centers, crypto mining, and AI facilities. It aims to shield residential and small business customers from higher utility bills tied to data center infrastructure. Now awaiting a Senate vote.

Michigan Senate Bills 761, 762, and 763 (Sens. Rosemary Bayer D-West Bloomfield, Sue Shink D-Northfield Township, Erika Geiss D-Taylor) introduced December 2025. SB 761 bans data centers withdrawing >2 million gallons/day for consumptive use (water that evaporates or doesn't return to systems). SB 762 requires the Michigan PSC to publish annual reports on total energy spending and water usage of data centers. SB 763 prohibits passing infrastructure improvement costs on to residential ratepayers. As of March 2026, none of the bills have passed out of committee. Separately, Reps. Wortz, Fox, and Wegela introduced a bipartisan three-bill House package (the 'Data Center Regulation Act') that would prohibit new data centers from receiving permits or commencing operations until April 1, 2027. Speaker Matt Hall and Gov. Whitmer have not commented, raising doubts about passage.

The Montana Right to Compute Act (SB 212), signed by Governor Gianforte on April 16, 2025, establishes a legal right to own and use computational resources including data centers. The law requires any government regulation restricting computing resources to be 'demonstrably necessary and narrowly tailored,' effectively limiting local governments' ability to restrict data center siting or operations through zoning. The law was enacted without significant organized opposition, representing a setback for community control advocates.

Mississippi SB 3168, enacted July 1, 2025, expands the state's data center tax incentive program. The law grants sales and use tax exemptions to data centers investing $250M+ and creating 35+ jobs at 125% of average state wages, adds electricity purchases to exempt 'state taxes,' provides automatic 10-year extension eligibility for qualifying expansions ($100M+), and excludes cryptocurrency mining. The bill passed both chambers and was signed into law without significant opposition.

Clean Cloud Act of 2025 (S. 1475), introduced by Senate Democrats, establishes an emissions standard and fee system for large data centers and cryptomining facilities. Beginning January 1, 2026, the EPA Administrator assesses fees on covered facilities. Region-specific emissions baselines reduce annually by 11% from 2026 through 2034, reaching net-zero by 2035. Facilities exceeding baselines pay a fee starting at $20/metric ton CO2e, rising annually by inflation plus $10. Annual data collection required for facilities with >100 kW IT power. Collected fees: 3% admin, 25% grants offsetting residential energy costs, 70% grants/loans for zero-carbon energy and long-duration storage. As of early 2026, the bill remains in committee with little movement.

Executive Order 14318, 'Accelerating Federal Permitting of Data Center Infrastructure,' was signed by President Trump on July 23, 2025. The order directs agencies to streamline NEPA reviews, establish new categorical exclusions for data center and energy projects, and designate qualifying projects ($500M+ capital or 100+ MW load) as 'transparency projects' for expedited FAST-41 review. It authorizes use of federal land for data centers, with DOE selecting four initial sites, and directs the Commerce Department to provide financial support including loans, grants, and tax incentives. The order was enacted without Congressional action.

Virginia HB 784 provides additional transparency by requiring an annual report disclosing information about each data center operator that receives a sales tax exemption, including whether they've met job and investment goals. Part of Virginia's massive 2026 legislative push: lawmakers considered 61 data center bills, sent 15 to the governor's desk, and carried over 46 to 2027. As of late March 2026, the data center tax exemption (~$1.6B/year) remains unresolved as the Senate seeks to eliminate it entirely while the House wants to tie it to environmental compliance. This budget standoff has delayed the state budget; a special session is set for April 23 to resolve the issue. Among bills passed: HB 507 (Tier IV generators required for new data centers, awaiting governor) and HB 1393 (rate-recovery for new generation costs from large electric demand customers).

Virginia SB 393, the Virginia's Great Outdoors Act (companion HB 641), was defeated in February 2026. The bill would have created a $250M/year dedicated funding stream for land conservation, public lands, trails, and outdoor recreation funded by a $3/sq ft annual tax on data centers above 25,000 sq ft. The Senate Finance Resources Subcommittee voted to 'carry over' SB 393, delaying it indefinitely. Both HB 641 and SB 393 have been defeated. The proposal would have established Virginia's largest-ever conservation fund, including a Virginia Tribal Commitment Fund, and was supported by PEC, NVCT, and conservation groups statewide.

Prohibits Virginia utilities from recovering costs of transmission lines and energy generation serving data centers over 100 MW from residential ratepayers. Aimed at addressing the $4.4 billion in data center transmission costs borne by customers across seven PJM states in 2024 alone. House subcommittee recommended continuing to next session. Part of a broader package of 2026 Virginia data center bills targeting ratepayer protections and industry accountability.

Illinois SB 2182, sponsored by Sen. Elgie Sims Jr. (D), would require data centers receiving state tax credits to enter into labor peace agreements with unions representing workers who operate and maintain critical systems. Under LPAs, employers agree not to interfere with unionization efforts while unions agree not to strike. The bill would add labor requirements to Illinois's existing Data Center Investment Program and is advancing through the Senate.

In Virginia, Henrico County's Board of Zoning Appeals unanimously denied Centra Logistics' vested rights claim for a data center complex on nearly 200 acres along Darbytown Road in the Varina district on Feb. 26, 2026. CEO David Wagner had filed the claim in October 2025, Arguing the company had a right to build under the pre-June 2025 rules. But a key factor was that Wagner did not purchase the site until November 2025 -- a month after filing his claim. The company had previously withdrawn its provisional use permit application. Henrico adopted new rules in June 2025 requiring all hyperscale data centers to go through public hearings. Centra may appeal to Henrico Circuit Court.

Reston-based developer WestDulles Properties filed an appeal in March 2026 of Chesterfield County staff's determination that a Conditional Use Permit (CUP) is required before its 170-acre data center development at 1500 Battery Brooke Parkway in the James River Industrial Center can be built. Chesterfield Planning Director Andy Gillies determined the project requires a CUP as of January 1, 2026 under the new zoning ordinance making data centers conditional uses in industrially zoned land. WestDulles argues no CUP is needed because the site is zoned I-3 Heavy Industrial, where data centers were a by-right use under the previous ordinance. The matter is slated to go before the Chesterfield Board of Zoning Appeals in May 2026. Mass-grading plan was received by the county in fall 2025.

Powhatan County, Virginia, Board of Supervisors voted 5-0 on Oct. 29, 2025 to approve rezoning of approximately 61 additional acres to expand the planned Province Group data center campus from 120 to 181 acres (1.5M to 2M sq ft). This came despite the Planning Commission's unanimous recommendation of denial on Oct. 7. The $2.7B campus near the Powhatan-Chesterfield line could eventually house up to four data center buildings requiring 300-400 MW of power and a new substation. Estimated annual tax revenue at full build-out: $9.7 million. The original 120-acre campus was approved 3-2 in October 2024.

Amazon proposed three 110-foot-tall data center buildings totaling nearly 1.1 million sq ft on 52 acres along Ashton Avenue near Manassas Mall, sandwiched between three schools and a townhome development. The Prince William County, Virginia, Planning Commission voted 6-1 in June 2024 to recommend denial due to noise, wildlife, and neighborhood proximity concerns. The Board of County Supervisors shelved the proposal indefinitely in October 2024 and it was deferred again in January 2025 to a date uncertain. Amazon maintains it still plans to build on the site but no hearing date is set.
opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County

properties.summary: DeKalb County, Tennessee, Commission is considering data center regulations proposed by County Mayor Matt Adcock after receiving an inquiry about locating a nuclear-powered data center in the county. The proposed resolution would prohibit data centers within 5,000 feet of residences, schools, daycares, parks, churches, commercial, or industrial establishments. Applicants would need permits with appeal rights. Commissioner Tom Chandler called the resolution 'a sham' arguing the 5,000-foot restriction makes permit approval impossible anywhere in the county. The action was tabled to allow public input; no final vote recorded.

Would restore local government control over data center siting in West Virginia, reversing HB 2014 — the Power Generation and Consumption Act signed by Gov. Morrisey — which allows data centers to operate on independent microgrids and strips counties of jurisdiction over siting and size. Tucker County residents pushed for the bill amid growing backlash over development in their communities. Never received a committee hearing during the session that ended March 14, 2026.
opposition_groups: West Virginia Center on Budget & Policy

properties.summary: The Virginia Senate budget proposes accelerating the data center tax exemption sunset from 2035 to January 2027, which would redirect $1.6B/year to tax rebates, transportation, and water infrastructure. The House budget preserves the exemption but adds clean energy requirements. The Virginia Data Center Reform Coalition, Piedmont Environmental Council, and Good Jobs First are pushing for the accelerated sunset. The General Assembly reconvenes April 22 to resolve the stalemate.

The Maricopa County, Arizona, Board of Supervisors unanimously approved a modernized zoning ordinance establishing specific rules for data center development, effective January 9, 2026. The new rules limit data centers to two industrial zoning districts (IND-2 and IND-3), requiring full rezoning for any other land.

In Colorado, CoreSite is building a 600,000-square-foot data center in Elyria-Swansea, One of the most polluted zip codes in the country (80216), which proceeded as a by-right use without community review. Hundreds packed a February 2026 meeting demanding transparency, and delivered a 'Good Neighbor' proposal. Denver's mayor subsequently joined calls for a moratorium.

Tucson is developing a Unified Development Code amendment to regulate data centers. The proposed amendment defines data centers, requires demonstration of adequate water supply, expands public notification requirements (half-mile radius vs. current 400 ft), and mandates developer-hosted neighborhood meetings. Public comment period closed end of March with strong participation. City leaders discussed proposed regulations at their April 6 study session. Dozens of residents turned out to oppose large-scale data centers at earlier public meetings.

Despite community opposition from RAZE and the Ak-Chin Indian Community, Pinal County, Arizona, approved three major data center projects in November 2025 totaling ~3,900 acres, including the $33 billion Vermaland 'La Osa' project near Eloy. Residents raised concerns about desert water use, noise, cultural impacts to tribal land, and infrastructure strain.

In Iron County, Utah, 350+ residents packed Festival Hall on March 5, 2026 for a hearing on the Antelope Data Center proposed for 640 acres on Antelope Springs Road, 15 miles NW of Cedar City. Of 15+ speakers over 2 hours, only one supported the project. Concerns: water (2.6M gallon initial fill), utility prices, farmland loss, noise, pollution.
opposition_groups: Iron County Citizens for Responsible Growth

properties.summary: Millard County, Utah, Commission approved rezoning of 4,400+ acres from agricultural to forest/range for Creekstone Energy solar farm to power AI data centers, despite resident opposition. Concerns about ruining land's multipurpose use, visual impact, and long-term consequences. The project involves 4400 acres. Opposition was led by Millard County Residents Against Solar Farm. Key concerns driving this action include environmental impacts and noise impacts.
opposition_groups: Millard County Residents Against Solar Farm

properties.summary: Two lawsuits filed against Doña Ana County, New Mexico, over 'Project Jupiter' — a 1,400-acre data center complex near Sunland Park with $165B in Industrial Revenue Bonds (by OpenAI). Empowerment Congress and NMELC allege Open Meetings Act violations and improper LEDA tax rebates. Project includes natural gas power plants and desalination plant. Could emit more greenhouse gases than Albuquerque and Las Cruces combined.
opposition_groups: Empowerment Congress of Doña Ana County

properties.summary: In Arizona, HB 2795 would prohibit counties (<500K pop) from denying zoning approval for small modular reactors after federal design certification and site permitting. Allows SMR construction colocated with large industrial users (data centers) without ACC environmental compatibility certificate. Cleared House on party-line vote Feb 26, advancing to Senate. Gov. Hobbs vetoed similar bill in 2025.

CyrusOne filed plans for a 400-megawatt data center campus in Whitney, Hill County, with an initial $750 million building due by October 2026. Residents packed a town hall citing water use from Lake Whitney (a petition with 6,000 signatures), noise, and tax abatements. The opposition has become a key issue in the 2026 Republican primary for Texas Senate District 22.

Amazon is building a 1-million-square-foot data center on 1,800 acres at the Keystone Trade Center in Falls Township, Bucks County, fast-tracked through Governor Shapiro's PA Permit Fast Track Program. Falls Township Residents Against Data Centers organized opposition, citing energy grid strain, increased electricity rates, and diesel generator impacts. A regional survey found 42% of SE Pennsylvania residents object to data centers vs 34% in support. The project was approved despite community concerns.
opposition_groups: Falls Township Residents Against Data Centers

properties.summary: Bryan, Texas, City Council proactively annexed 162 acres near the RELLIS campus and adopted a Planned Development zoning district allowing data centers with stricter-than-normal standards including increased setbacks, noise requirements, and screening specifications. No data center project has been formally proposed yet. The preemptive zoning measures represent a community win in establishing regulations before development pressure arrives.

The Oneonta, New York, Town Board passed a 12-month moratorium on AI data centers in March 2026 after residents presented more than 700 signatures, prompted by Eco-Yotta Inc.'s plans to rezone 150 acres of farmland. This came weeks after the nearby Town of Dryden instituted an outright ban following its own 18-month moratorium.

Verdantas/Shelbourne proposed an 847,000 sq ft data center complex on a 44-acre site at the White Clay Center Industrial Park off Ogletown Road in Newark, DE. The proposal would demolish seven existing buildings and replace them with a three-building data center campus. Site is owned by NY-based Shelbourne (8M sq ft commercial real estate portfolio). The location is outside Delaware's Coastal Zone and is in zoning that already allows data center projects. Critically, the New Castle County Council passed new data center regulations in March 2026 that explicitly do NOT apply to the White Clay project (or other in-pipeline proposals) — only to newly proposed projects. The project remains in early stages of approval.

Grant County Commissioners passed a 24-month moratorium on data centers on March 2, 2026. The Area Plan Commission began drafting ordinance language focusing on transparency, resource impacts, and local benefits, including questions about client disclosure and annual energy/water reporting. Opposition was led by Citizens Action Coalition of Indiana. The community successfully achieved its objective.

795-acre data center (Hillwood/PowerHouse) on south Joliet farmland above depleting aquifer. Plan Commission approved 7-1 despite 4+ hours of opposition testimony. Hundreds packed City Hall. Would be largest data center in Illinois. Plan Commission approved 7-1 on March 5. At the March 17 City Council meeting, a marathon 6.5-hour public hearing forced a recess -- vote delayed to Thursday March 19 due to election day restrictions. Developers promise $100M community contribution, 7,000-10,000 construction jobs, and 700 permanent jobs. Would be the largest data center in Illinois at $20B, 1.8GW, 795 acres. Joliet City Council approved the $20B PowerHouse/Hillwood campus 8-1 on March 20 after a 6.5-hour public hearing. Only Councilmember Suzanna Ibarra voted no.

Lake County, Indiana, Council approved stringent data center regulations: only permitted in industrial zones with special approval, 200-ft setbacks from residential/agricultural, 55dB noise max, underground utilities, decommissioning plans, and annual firefighter training requirements. The project involves 160 acres. Opposition was led by Citizens Action Coalition and Hoosier Action. Key concerns driving this action include zoning concerns. The community successfully achieved its objective.

Sen. Kent Smith (D-Euclid) introduced a package of 6+ bills targeting data center impacts in Ohio: the Data Center Grid Cost Responsibility Act, a Tax Break Ban, the Powering Ohio Communities Act, the Local Development Protection Act, the Power Reliability and Fairness Act, and the Responsible Water Use Act which would cap daily water use at 5 million gallons. The Ohio Consumers' Counsel endorsed the package. The bills are pending committee action.

Thomas County, Georgia, Board of Commissioners unanimously approved a 12-month moratorium on AI data center construction in January 2026. Commission Chair Mark NeSmith said the board was 'certainly not anti-data centers' but preferred to be proactive in protecting the community while gathering more information.

Google proposed a 1GW data center on 546 acres near Irwinville in an agricultural zone. The Irwin County, Georgia, Planning Commission voted to recommend approval on Feb 26, 2026. However, the applicant withdrew the application on March 2, 2026 before the County Commission could vote, without prejudice to filing a similar application in the future. Opposition was led by Suwannee Riverkeeper/WWALS Watershed Coalition and concerned residents who raised alarms about water usage (1M+ gallons/day), impacts on the Alapaha River, noise, and farmland loss. Following the withdrawal, Irwin County began developing a data center ordinance, with a public hearing held March 17.

Clarksdale, Mississippi, Board of Commissioners voted on March 24, 2026, to rezone a 648-acre site between Sunflower River and Roundyard neighborhood from commercial/agricultural to light industrial for a potential data center. The rezoning came with three conditions: a one-time $5 million impact fee, the developer paying for all public utility improvements, and a 1,000-foot setback from residential areas. Earlier board meetings in January were contentious, with the board tabling the vote after a failed motion. No developer has yet filed an application. The site lies along U.S. 278 and Tallahatchie Street, and the vote is described as only 'the beginning of the conversation' about data centers in Clarksdale.

Alabama HB 399, sponsored by Rep. Hulsey (R-Helena), is the House companion to SB 265. Limits tax abatements for data center projects over $200M to 20 years, down from the current 30-year maximum. Requires data centers consuming 100+ MW to pay state sales and use taxes allocated to the General Fund, effective January 1, 2027. Passed House 103-0. Part of a bipartisan effort to rein in data center subsidies after the Meridian Compass deal was named '2025 Worst Economic Development Deal.'

Nobles County Planning and Zoning Commission voted on April 8, 2026 against changing the zoning ordinance to allow data centers as conditional use in agricultural preservation areas, blocking Geronimo Power's proposed data center in Elk Township. After 2+ hours of discussion, 19 spoke in support and 18 against. The Nobles County Board of Commissioners will have the final say.
opposition_groups: Nobles County Residents Against Data Centers

properties.summary: The Minnesota Center for Environmental Advocacy (MCEA) filed an appeal against the City of Faribault over an 'inadequate' environmental review of Archer Datacenters' proposal on 87.6 acres. MCEA alleges the developer dropped estimated greenhouse gas emissions by 98% between draft and final stages without explanation, and failed to analyze cumulative impacts from the wave of nearby data center proposals. The appeal is active and pending resolution.

Prince William County, Virginia, Board of Supervisors voted to eliminate by-right approval for data centers in the Data Center Opportunity Zone Overlay District, requiring special use permits for all future facilities. Republican Supervisor Tom Gordy said the overlay 'has outlived its useful purpose' and suggested ending it outright. The same meeting controversially approved the Hornbaker Road data center outside the overlay in a 4-3 vote despite heated public opposition.
opposition_groups: Coalition to Protect Prince William County

properties.summary: Culpeper County, Virginia, unanimously required conditional use permits for data centers on industrial land in September 2025, impacting 3,000+ acres across 140+ parcels that previously allowed by-right construction. Five projects in the Culpeper Technology Zone remain vested. The Piedmont Environmental Council and Coalition to Save Culpeper pushed for the stronger regulations.

GigaLand LLC resubmitted a scaled-down data center proposal near Remington after withdrawing the original in September 2025, reducing building space by 55% (from 2.2M to 998,662 sq ft) and cutting substation capacity from 1.2 GW to 600 MW, with a $3 billion estimated cost on 202 acres. On April 7, 2026, the Fauquier County Board of Supervisors adopted two resolutions targeting Dominion Energy: (1) opposing the Valley Link Joshua Falls to Yeat transmission line project, and (2) demanding Dominion bury transmission lines and reveal its high-load clients (data centers). The Planning Commission had previously recommended denial of the Morrisville substation expansion (4-1 vote).

Authorizes a federal study on the environmental, economic, and public health impacts of data centers, with a focus on communities of color and low-income communities. Requires the Secretary of Energy to conduct the study and report findings to Congress. Endorsed by NRDC, Climate Justice Alliance, GreenLatinos, Food & Water Watch.

In Iowa, originally filed as HF 2447, Renumbered to HF 2690 after committee approval on Feb 23, 2026. Amendment H-8214 filed March 12. Key provisions: quarterly water/energy reporting, public disclosure within 30 days, separate utility rate class for data centers using 20+ MW. Sponsored by Rep. Cindy Golding (R-Cedar Rapids). First-of-its-kind legislation targeting water utilities specifically.

The PRICE Act (Preventing Rate Inflation in Consumer Energy), HR6983, introduced by Reps. Rob Menendez (D-NJ) and Greg Casar (D-TX), would require data center operators to generate all electricity on-site rather than drawing from the grid. The bill mandates 75% renewable energy by 2035 and 100% by 2040, with civil penalties up to $100,000 per day for violations. Endorsed by the Consumer Federation of America and Evergreen Action, the companion Data Center Transparency Act (HR6984) would require EPA and EIA to collect quarterly environmental and energy data from every US data center.

HR 6179, the Clean Cloud Act of 2025, was introduced by Rep. Steve Cohen (D-TN) — the House companion to S.1475 by Senate Democrats. Establishes emission standards for high-demand energy users (data centers and cryptominers). Beginning 2026, mandates collection of detailed electricity usage and emissions data from facilities >100 kW installed power. Beginning 2026, fees on electricity utilities serving these facilities based on GHG emissions, base rate $20/ton above regional baseline, increasing annually by inflation plus $10. Region-specific baselines reduce 11%/year through 2034 reaching net-zero by 2035. Referred to House Energy and Commerce Committee. Bill remains in committee with little movement as of early 2026. Companion to Sen. Whitehouse's S.1475.

HR 5227, the Unleashing Low-Cost Rural AI Act (Rep. Moore), would direct the Secretary of Energy to designate a National Laboratory to study the impact of AI and data center site growth on US energy supply resources. Study would identify rural areas poised for tech expansions and assess impact on energy supply, reliability, and consumer costs. Examines whether infrastructure updates are needed to support co-location of AI/data centers, and feasibility of using alternative energy sources (hydroelectric, solar, wind, battery storage, carbon capture, nuclear, geothermal). Bill is in 119th Congress (2025-2026). Awaiting committee action.

DOE Section 403 directive: On October 23, 2025, US DOE Secretary Chris Wright issued a formal directive to FERC under Section 403 of the DOE Organization Act to initiate rulemaking for large load interconnection. FERC issued an ANOPR titled 'Ensuring the Timely and Orderly Interconnection of Large Loads' (Docket RM26-4). The rule would standardize interconnection procedures for data centers and other large loads >20 MW, with 100% participant funding proposed (data center developers pay full cost of grid network upgrades). DOE Secretary Wright directed FERC to finalize by April 30, 2026. ~200 comments received. States and utilities oppose federal jurisdiction expansion.

FERC issued an order in December 2025 directing PJM, the nation's largest grid operator, to establish transparent rules to facilitate service of AI-driven data centers and other large loads co-located with generating facilities. The Commission found PJM's tariff was unjust and unreasonable due to inadequate co-location rules. The order proposes new transmission service types (Firm Contract Demand, Non-Firm Contract Demand, and interim service). Response briefs were due March 18, 2026, and reply briefs are due April 17, 2026 (Docket No. EL25-49-000).

New York S6394A, the Sustainable Data Centers Act, would regulate energy consumption by data centers in the state. The bill requires annual disclosure reporting, prohibits fossil fuel power purchase agreements, and directs the Public Service Commission to establish a surcharge/discount plan. It mandates a progressive transition to renewable energy: 33% by 2030, 67% by 2035, and 100% by 2040. The bill is pending committee action.

New York S8540, the Accountability for Computing and Data Centers (ACDC) Act, would require electric and gas corporations to establish an independent classification of service for large energy use facilities separate from other rate classes. The bill authorizes DPS to develop financial surety requirements and prevents data center costs from being blended into residential rate cases. The bill is pending in the Senate.

In New Hampshire, HB 1724, Sponsored by Rep. Wendy Thomas, Requires public utilities to submit annual reports detailing transmission costs, wholesale electricity market impacts, and generation capacity adequacy, with specific analysis of data center-related demand. Mandates public hearings and review by the state energy agency. Referred to the House Science, Technology and Energy Committee with a public hearing held February 10, 2026.

Wyoming HB0090 would have appropriated $500K to the State Engineer to study how large-scale industrial water use by data centers, carbon capture, and hydrogen projects impacts the state's water supply. The bill advanced 8-1 out of the House Agriculture Committee and received House Appropriations Committee approval on February 16, 2026, but did not pass the full chamber. The study was part of growing concern in Wyoming as data center developers eye the state for its cheap land and energy resources.

In Mississippi, HB 1635 was introduced to redirect 80% of local ad-valorem taxes collected above $1B from large projects (including data centers) into a new state infrastructure and economic development fund, with 20% to local government and schools. Madison County Economic Development Authority director Joey Deason objected to the measure, warning it would slash his county's projected $70M/year in new Amazon data center tax revenue to just $20M/year. The author did not bring the bill to committee vote.

Menlo Equities proposed a data center on 40.6 acres in the Ahwatukee, Arizona, Foothills area of Phoenix. The Ahwatukee Foothills Residents Against Data Centers organized opposition, citing concerns about water use in the desert, noise, and environmental impacts on the residential community. The project remains ongoing with the outcome pending.
opposition_groups: Ahwatukee Foothills Residents Against Data Centers

properties.summary: Tract is developing the 2,019-acre Buckeye Technology Park, the largest contiguous data center project in the Phoenix market, located near Palo Verde nuclear power plant and the I-10 corridor in Buckeye, AZ. Could see up to 20 million sq ft of data center space across as many as 40 individual data centers at full buildout. Tract is currently working with the local utility on plans to support up to 1.8 GW of capacity. $20 billion total investment over a 15-year buildout, currently in planning stage. Tract acquired the 2,069-acre site from Arizona Land Consulting for $136M+ (which had bought it for $40M in 2022). Buckeye is considering a development agreement reimbursing Tract up to $50M in public infrastructure improvements (water, sewer, streets, transportation), funded through 20% of tax revenues over 20 years.
opposition_groups: Buckeye Residents Against Data Centers (ad hoc)

properties.summary: Oppidan proposed a 5MW data center on a 7-acre site in the Stead area of Reno, Nevada,. The Sierra Club Toiyabe Chapter raised environmental, grid, and water concerns. Despite opposition, the project was approved, with community advocates failing to block the permit. The community's stated objective was to deny Oppidan 5MW data center permit in Reno.

DCPC's Summit Ridge Data Center, a 100 MW off-grid natural gas-powered facility in Santaquin, Utah, was approved by City Council in Oct 2024 without adequate public awareness. Residents rallied outside City Hall, feeling 'blindsided.' A Change.org petition has 1,350+ signatures. Concerns about air quality from natural gas generators, noise, and lack of transparency.

Lowell City Council voted 10-0 to enact a one-year moratorium on data center development -- the first such moratorium in Massachusetts. Championed by community group 'Honest Future for Lowell.' Aims to give officials time to review zoning rules and understand infrastructure impacts. Markley Group operates an existing data center and plans expansion.

Gibraltar, Michigan, City Council unanimously approved a year-long moratorium in March 2026, freezing a proposed 100 MW data center at the former McLouth Steel site (27800 W. Jefferson Ave.) by Raeden. The facility would consume as much energy as 80,000 homes. Residents and Downriver activists raised concerns about increased electric bills and impacts on nearby Humbug Marsh wildlife refuge. The developer proposed addressing contamination at an adjacent Superfund property and partnering with DTE Energy for on-site power generation.

Scioto Township trustees in Pickaway County enacted a 12-month moratorium on data center development after a heated public meeting (by EdgeConneX). Trustees debated duration, ultimately agreeing a full year was necessary to craft legally sound zoning regulations. Concerns centered on rising residential electricity costs, environmental strain from water usage, and noise pollution. Part of a broader wave across Ohio where generous sales tax exemptions made rural communities prime targets.

The Village of DeForest, Wisconsin, announced on January 27 that QTS Data Centers' proposed $12 billion data center was 'not feasible.' The project required annexing 1,600 acres from the Town of Vienna, but a state Department of Administration report concluded the annexation was not in the public interest, citing concerns about water and sewer services. Village staff recommended rejection and QTS subsequently withdrew.

Constellation Energy purchased over 600 acres near Essex, Illinois, and is seeking rezoning and annexation. Much of the land was sold by village board members or their relatives. The Essex Coalition launched a petition urging industrial protections and is working to revive the village's dormant zoning board of appeals. Constellation has not officially announced a data center but residents fear one is planned given the site's proximity to power infrastructure.

About two dozen south and southwest suburban residents gathered at a Frankfort, Illinois, forum organized by environmental advocates to voice concerns about data center proposals in Joliet, Minooka, Essex, Coal City, and Yorkville. Residents fear higher utility bills, regional power outages, declining air quality, and strained water supplies from the cluster of proposed facilities in the greater Will County area.
opposition_groups: Sierra Club Illinois (Heart of Illinois Group)

properties.summary: Kosciusko County, Indiana, Area Plan Commission unanimously denied (8-0) Prologis's rezoning request for 554 acres of farmland near Leesburg from agricultural to industrial for a 10-building data center campus. County Commissioners upheld denial 3-0 on April 22. Over 100 residents attended, and a petition collected 1,000+ signatures. Residents cited loss of farmland and impact on the local Amish farming community.

Washington Township in northwestern Franklin County passed a 90-day moratorium on data centers in unincorporated parts of the township on December 8, 2025. The township also asked the city of Dublin, which the township encompasses, to adopt a moratorium as well. The action follows the broader wave of Central Ohio communities pushing back against the data center boom centered on New Albany.
